Report Sections

  • About This Report
  • Study Overview
  • This study is guided by 3 theories: Social Learning Theory, Family Systems, and Attachment Theory, which explains how individual leanr through imitating behaviors they observe. This ultimately affects how they navigate social relationships, such as potential romantic relationships. One behavior they pick up is their attachment style in which they react to emotional bonds and conflict. While prior theory lays much groundwork, there isn’t much knowledge regarding people that are single and actively seeking out relationships through other platforms, such as dating apps. This study aims to close the gap by investigating the role of attachment security on the effect of high vs. low communication quality shown through dating profiles.
    • Setup and Software Environment
  • Packages including tidyverse, psych, afex, and emmeans were downloaded to assist the flow of running the data analysis.
    • Data Source and Analysis Dataset
  • Data was collected for 1 week, then imported into R for analysis.
  • The output here presents scores from a total of 94 participants from all 3 scales (ETRR, IAS, & AAS), to measure perceived compatability, interpersonal attraction, and attachment.
  • The output here defines the scale items.
    • Variable Preparation
  • Output here prepares participant ID and the condition they were given
  • 45 participants were given the low communication condition, 50 participants were given the high communication condition
  • The output provides a dataset comprised 95 participants and 52 variables, including a table representing the 2×2 experimental design: High vs. Low Communication × High vs. Low Attachment Security, demographic controls (age, gender, relationship), and 3 multi-item self-report scales: the Interpersonal Attraction Scale (IAS_1–IAS_10), the Adult Attachment Scale (AAS_1–AAS_18), and the Expectations Toward Romatinc Relationship Scale (ETTR_1–ETTR_19).
  • For the IAS Scale, items 8, 11, 16, and 18 were reverse-coded
  • For the ETRR Scale, items 3, 4, 8, and 9 were reverse-coded
  • For the AAS Scale, items 6, 9, 10, 13, 16, 18 were reverse-coded
  • Reverse-coded items were checked by comparing each original item against its recoded counterpart for the first 10 participants. The transformations performed as expected.
  • For the IAS Scale, the AAS Scale along with multiple subscales including: closeness, dependence, anxiety, and total attachment, and ETRR Scale. Output displays Min., 1st Quartile, Median, Mean, 3rd Quartile, Max., and NA’s
  • The different subscales for attachment are created here, including: closeness, dependence, anxiety, and total attachment.
  • Total: lower attachment security group had 49 participants, higher attachment security group had 46 participants
  • Closenss: lower closeness security group had 48 participants, higher closeness security group had 47 participants
  • Dependence: lower dependence security group had 50 participants, higher dependence security group had 45 participants
  • Anxiety: lower anxiety security group had 51 participants, higher anxiety security group had 44 participants
  • To check for reliability regarding the scales, the data frames were loaded in here to compute internal consistency
  • Internal consistency values are reported above, reporting Cronbach’s alpha and McDonald’s omega
    • Descriptive Statistics
  • Descriptive statistics regarding the scale scores are recorded here
  • The output describes the values for each scale, showing the vars., total participants, standard deviation, median, trimmed, mad, min., max, range, skew, kurtosis, and se
  • The output here describes the age range of particiapnts, which ranged from 18 - 24, gender of the participants, and if they are in a relationship or not
    • Correlations Among the DVs
  • Intercorrelations among the six composite scales are presented above. The dependent variables measured through the IAS and ETRR Scale were strongly correlated. The AAS subscales showed the expected pattern: Close and Depend security were positively intercorrelated, and both were negatively correlated with Anxiety security.
    • Primary Analyses
  • ANOVA perfomed on relationship expectation scores
  • The main effect of communication condition was significant
  • The main effect of attachment security groups was not significant
  • The interaction main effect between communication and attachment security was not significant
  • The results are averages over the levels of condition
  • For the low condition lower attachment security, the estimated mean is 3.28
  • For the low condition higher attachment security, the estimated mean is 3.42
  • For the high condition lower attachment security, the estimated mean is 4.23
  • For the high condition higher attachment security, the estimated mean is 4.52
  • The estimated means for the high condition group is gnerally higher than the low condition group
  • The main effect of communication condition is significant
  • The main effect of attachment close security groups was not significant
  • The interaction main effect between communication and attachment was not significant
  • The results are averages over the levels of condition
  • For the low condition lower closeness security, the estimated mean is 3.19
  • For the low condition higher closeness security, the estimated mean is 3.48
  • For the high condition lower closeness security, the estimated mean is 4.26
  • For the high condition higher closeness security, the estimated mean is 4.48
  • The estimated means for the high condition group is gnerally higher than the low condition group
  • The main effect of communication condition was significant
  • The main effect of attachment dependence security groups was not significant
  • The interaction main effect between communication and attachment depedence was not significant
  • The results are averages over the levels of condition
  • For the low condition lower dependence security, the estimated mean is 3.31
  • For the low condition higher dependence security, the estimated mean is 3.39
  • For the high condition lower dependence security, the estimated mean is 4.18
  • For the high condition higher dependence security, the estimated mean is 4.47
    • # The estimated means for the high condition group is gnerally higher than the low condition group
  • The output here shows a significant main effect of communication condition
  • The main effect of attachment anxiety condition was significant
  • The interaction main effect between communication and attachment anxiety was not significant
  • The results are averages over the levels of condition
  • For the low condition lower anxiety security, the estimated mean is 3.24
  • For the low condition higher anxiety security, the estimated mean is 3.53
  • For the high condition lower anxiety security, the estimated mean is 4.25
  • For the high condition higher anxiety security, the estimated mean is 4.46
  • The estimated means for the high condition group is gnerally higher than the low condition group
  • The output here shows a significant main effect of communication condition
  • The main effect of attachment security condition was significant
  • The interaction main effect between communication and attachment security was not significant
  • The results are averages over the levels of condition
  • For the low condition lower attachment security, the estimated mean is 4.44
  • For the low condition higher attachment security, the estimated mean is 5.14
  • For the high condition lower attachment security, the estimated mean is 5.42
  • For the high condition higher attachment security, the estimated mean is 5.86
  • The estimated means for the high condition group is gnerally higher than the low condition group
  • The output here shows a significant main effect of communication condition
  • The main effect of attachment closeness condition was significant
  • The interaction main effect between communication and attachment closeness was not significant
  • The results are averages over the levels of condition
  • For the low condition lower closeness security, the estimated mean is 4.56
  • For the low condition higher closeness security, the estimated mean is 5.04
  • For the high condition lower closeness security, the estimated mean is 5.42
  • For the high condition higher closeness security, the estimated mean is 5.86
  • The estimated means for the high condition group is gnerally higher than the low condition group
  • The output here shows a significant main effect of communication condition
  • The main effect of attachment dependent condition was significant
  • The interaction main effect between communication and attachment dependent was not significant
  • The results are averages over the levels of condition
  • For the low condition lower dependence security, the estimated mean is 4.47
  • For the low condition higher dependence security, the estimated mean is 5.17
  • For the high condition lower dependence security, the estimated mean is 5.38
  • For the high condition higher dependence security, the estimated mean is 5.88
  • The estimated means for the high condition group is gnerally higher than the low condition group
  • The output here shows a significant main effect of communication condition
  • The main effect of attachment anxiety condition was significant
  • The interaction main effect between communication and attachment anxiety was not significant
  • The results are averages over the levels of condition
  • For the low condition lower anxiety security, the estimated mean is 4.56
  • For the low condition higher anxiety security, the estimated mean is 5.19
  • For the high condition lower anxiety security, the estimated mean is 5.38
  • For the high condition higher anxiety security, the estimated mean is 5.83
  • The estimated means for the high condition group is gnerally higher than the low condition group
    • Figures
  • Below are the bar graphs for each measure along with the subscale scores
  • There does not appear to be an interaction
  • There does not appear to be an interaction
  • There does not appeaer to be an interaction
  • There does not appeaer to be an interaction
  • There does not appeaer to be an interaction
  • There does not appeaer to be an interaction
  • There does not appeaer to be an interaction
  • There appears to be an interaction
    • Results Summary
  • While there are multipel ANOVAs conducted in this statistical analysis report, this study only looks at communication x attachment security on perceived compatability and communication x attachment security on social attraction.
  • For the first ANOVA, the results revealed a significant main effect of communication, no significant main effect of attachment security, and no significant interaction effect.
  • For the second ANOVA, the results revealed a significant main effect of communication, a significant main effect of attachment security, but no significant interaction effect.
    • Reproducibility Information
  • This specific R version, operating system, and package versions were employed to compile the statistical analysis reporty in this section. Sharing these results allows for readers to recreate the exact computational context in which the analyses were performed.

The Role of Attachment Style in Perceived Compatibility and Social Attraction in Romantic Relationships : Effects of High vs. Low Communication in Dating Profiles

  • Show All Code
  • Hide All Code
  • View Source

PSY 120L Reproducible Analysis Report

Author

Kaylene Truong

Published

June 7, 2026

About This Report

#This study looks at the effect of communication, moderated by attachmet style, perceived compatability and social attraction. While there are multiple ANOVA models including several useful main effects, however for this study, we will be looking mainly at the ANOVA predicting relationship expectation scores and social attraction scores.

Study Overview

This study is guided by 3 theories: Social Learning Theory, Family Systems, and Attachment Theory, which explains how individual leanr through imitating behaviors they observe. This ultimately affects how they navigate social relationships, such as potential romantic relationships. One behavior they pick up is their attachment style in which they react to emotional bonds and conflict. While prior theory lays much groundwork, there isn’t much knowledge regarding people that are single and actively seeking out relationships through other platforms, such as dating apps. This study aims to close the gap by investigating the role of attachment security on the effect of high vs. low communication quality shown through dating profiles.

Setup and Software Environment

Packages including tidyverse, psych, afex, and emmeans were downloaded to assist the flow of running the data analysis.

Load Packages

Show code 
library(tidyverse)  # data manipulation + plotting
library(psych)      # scale construction + reliability
library(afex)       # ANOVA models + effect sizes
library(emmeans)    # estimated marginal means + confidence intervals

Data Source and Analysis Dataset

Data was collected for 1 week, then imported into R for analysis.

Import Dataset

Show code 
study_raw <- read_csv("data/relationship_preferences_data.csv")

Check Imported Dataset

Show code 
dim(study_raw)
[1] 95 66
Show code

The output here presents scores from a total of 94 participants from all 3 scales (ETRR, IAS, & AAS), to measure perceived compatability, interpersonal attraction, and attachment.

Create Analysis Dataset

Show code 
study_vars <- c(
  
      "condition",  
      "age", "gender", "relationship",
  
      "IAS_1","IAS_2","IAS_3","IAS_4", "IAS_5","IAS_6",
      "IAS_7","IAS_8","IAS_9","IAS_10", 
      
      "AAS_1","AAS_2","AAS_3","AAS_4","AAS_5","AAS_6","AAS_7",
      "AAS_8","AAS_9","AAS_10","AAS_11","AAS_12", "AAS_13","AAS_14",
      "AAS_15","AAS_16","AAS_17","AAS_18",  
      
      "ETTR_1", "ETTR_2", "ETTR_3", "ETTR_4", "ETTR_5", "ETTR_6", 
      "ETTR_7", "ETTR_8", "ETTR_9", "ETTR_10", "ETTR_11", "ETTR_12", 
      "ETTR_13", "ETTR_14", "ETTR_15", "ETTR_16", "ETTR_17", "ETTR_18", 
      "ETTR_19"
)


study_data <- study_raw %>%
  select(all_of(study_vars)) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), as.numeric))

Check Analysis Dataset

The output here defines the scale items.

Variable Preparation

add snippet text here

Prepare Participant ID and Experimental Condition

Show code 
study_data <- study_data %>%
  mutate(
    id = row_number(),
    condition = factor(
      condition,
      levels = c(0, 1),
      labels = c("Low", "High")
    )
  )

Output here prepares participant ID and the condition they were given

Check Experimental Condition Coding

Show code 
study_data %>%
  count(condition) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("Condition", "n")
  )
Condition n
Low 45
High 50

45 participants were given the low communication condition, 50 participants were given the high communication condition

Check Prepared Analysis Dataset

Show code 
dim(study_data)
[1] 95 52
Show code 
names(study_data)
 [1] "condition"    "age"          "gender"       "relationship" "IAS_1"       
 [6] "IAS_2"        "IAS_3"        "IAS_4"        "IAS_5"        "IAS_6"       
[11] "IAS_7"        "IAS_8"        "IAS_9"        "IAS_10"       "AAS_1"       
[16] "AAS_2"        "AAS_3"        "AAS_4"        "AAS_5"        "AAS_6"       
[21] "AAS_7"        "AAS_8"        "AAS_9"        "AAS_10"       "AAS_11"      
[26] "AAS_12"       "AAS_13"       "AAS_14"       "AAS_15"       "AAS_16"      
[31] "AAS_17"       "AAS_18"       "ETTR_1"       "ETTR_2"       "ETTR_3"      
[36] "ETTR_4"       "ETTR_5"       "ETTR_6"       "ETTR_7"       "ETTR_8"      
[41] "ETTR_9"       "ETTR_10"      "ETTR_11"      "ETTR_12"      "ETTR_13"     
[46] "ETTR_14"      "ETTR_15"      "ETTR_16"      "ETTR_17"      "ETTR_18"     
[51] "ETTR_19"      "id"

The output provides a dataset comprised 95 participants and 52 variables, including a table representing the 2×2 experimental design: High vs. Low Communication × High vs. Low Attachment Security, demographic controls (age, gender, relationship), and 3 multi-item self-report scales: the Interpersonal Attraction Scale (IAS_1–IAS_10), the Adult Attachment Scale (AAS_1–AAS_18), and the Expectations Toward Romatinc Relationship Scale (ETTR_1–ETTR_19).

Reverse-Code Scale Items

For the IAS Scale, items 8, 11, 16, and 18 were reverse-coded

For the ETRR Scale, items 3, 4, 8, and 9 were reverse-coded

For the AAS Scale, items 6, 9, 10, 13, 16, 18 were reverse-coded

Show code 
study_data <- study_data %>%
  mutate(
    IAS_3r = 8 - IAS_3,
    IAS_4r = 8 - IAS_4,
    IAS_5r = 8 - IAS_5,
    
    ETTR_2r  = 7 - ETTR_2,
    ETTR_5r  = 7 - ETTR_5,
    ETTR_7r  = 7 - ETTR_7,
    ETTR_10r = 7 - ETTR_10,
    ETTR_13r = 7 - ETTR_13,
    ETTR_16r = 7 - ETTR_16,
    ETTR_18r = 7 - ETTR_18,
    
    AAS_8r  = 6 - AAS_8,
    AAS_13r = 6 - AAS_13,
    AAS_17r = 6 - AAS_17,
    AAS_2r  = 6 - AAS_2,
    AAS_16r = 6 - AAS_16,
    AAS_18r = 6 - AAS_18,
    
    AAS_3r  = 6 - AAS_3,
    AAS_4r  = 6 - AAS_4,
    AAS_9r  = 6 - AAS_9,
    AAS_10r = 6 - AAS_10,
    AAS_11r = 6 - AAS_11,
    AAS_15r = 6 - AAS_15
  )

Check Reverse Coding

Show code 
study_data %>%
  select(IAS_3,IAS_3r, ETTR_2, ETTR_2r, AAS_3, AAS_3r) %>%
  head(10) %>%
  knitr::kable()
IAS_3 IAS_3r ETTR_2 ETTR_2r AAS_3 AAS_3r
4 4 3 4 1 5
2 6 2 5 3 3
4 4 2 5 2 4
3 5 3 4 1 5
3 5 4 3 1 5
6 2 3 4 5 1
2 6 5 2 3 3
4 4 4 3 2 4
7 1 2 5 3 3
4 4 4 3 3 3

Reverse-coded items were checked by comparing each original item against its recoded counterpart for the first 10 participants. The transformations performed as expected.

Create Scale Scores

add snippet here

Note: For this analysis workflow, the AAS items were reverse-scored so that all attachment scale scores point in a positive direction. Higher scores indicate more adaptive attachment-related outcomes. Specifically, higher total AAS scores indicate higher overall attachment security, higher closeness scores indicate greater comfort with closeness, higher dependence scores indicate greater comfort depending on others, and higher anxiety-security scores indicate lower attachment anxiety.

Show code 
# Define IAS social attraction items
IAS_items <- c("IAS_1", "IAS_2", "IAS_3r", "IAS_4r", "IAS_5r", "IAS_6")

# Define revised AAS close, depend, and anxiety-security items
aas_close_items <- c("AAS_1", "AAS_6", "AAS_8r", "AAS_12", "AAS_13r", "AAS_17r")

aas_depend_items <- c("AAS_2r", "AAS_5", "AAS_7", "AAS_14", "AAS_16r", "AAS_18r")

aas_anxiety_items <- c("AAS_3r", "AAS_4r", "AAS_9r", "AAS_10r", "AAS_11r", "AAS_15r")

aas_total_items <- c(aas_close_items, aas_depend_items, aas_anxiety_items)

# Define ETRR relationship expectation items
ETTR_items <- c("ETTR_1", "ETTR_2r", "ETTR_3", "ETTR_4", "ETTR_5r", "ETTR_6", 
                "ETTR_7r", "ETTR_8", "ETTR_9", "ETTR_10r", "ETTR_11", "ETTR_12", 
                "ETTR_13r", "ETTR_14", "ETTR_15", "ETTR_16r", "ETTR_17", "ETTR_18r", "ETTR_19")

study_data <- study_data %>%
  mutate(
    IAS_scale = rowMeans(across(all_of(IAS_items)), na.rm = TRUE),
    aas_close_scale = rowMeans(across(all_of(aas_close_items)), na.rm = TRUE),
    aas_depend_scale = rowMeans(across(all_of(aas_depend_items)), na.rm = TRUE),
    aas_anxiety_scale = rowMeans(across(all_of(aas_anxiety_items)), na.rm = TRUE),
    aas_total_scale = rowMeans(across(all_of(aas_total_items)), na.rm = TRUE),
    ETTR_scale = rowMeans(across(all_of(ETTR_items)), na.rm = TRUE)
  )

Check Scale Scores

Show code 
study_data %>%
  select(IAS_scale, aas_close_scale, aas_depend_scale, aas_anxiety_scale, aas_total_scale, ETTR_scale) %>%
  summary()
   IAS_scale     aas_close_scale aas_depend_scale aas_anxiety_scale
 Min.   :1.667   Min.   :1.000   Min.   :1.167    Min.   :1.000    
 1st Qu.:4.833   1st Qu.:2.833   1st Qu.:2.333    1st Qu.:2.367    
 Median :5.333   Median :3.333   Median :2.833    Median :3.167    
 Mean   :5.243   Mean   :3.361   Mean   :2.845    Mean   :3.166    
 3rd Qu.:6.000   3rd Qu.:3.833   3rd Qu.:3.417    3rd Qu.:3.833    
 Max.   :7.000   Max.   :5.000   Max.   :4.833    Max.   :5.000    
 NA's   :1                                                         
 aas_total_scale   ETTR_scale   
 Min.   :1.333   Min.   :1.789  
 1st Qu.:2.587   1st Qu.:3.053  
 Median :3.111   Median :4.053  
 Mean   :3.124   Mean   :3.882  
 3rd Qu.:3.611   3rd Qu.:4.632  
 Max.   :4.611   Max.   :5.895
Show code 
study_data %>%
  summarise(
    IAS_missing = mean(is.na(IAS_scale)),
    aas_close_missing = mean(is.na(aas_close_scale)),
    aas_depend_missing = mean(is.na(aas_depend_scale)),
    aas_anxiety_missing = mean(is.na(aas_anxiety_scale)),
    aas_total_missing = mean(is.na(aas_total_scale)),
    ETTR_missing = mean(is.na(ETTR_scale))
  ) %>%
  knitr::kable()
IAS_missing aas_close_missing aas_depend_missing aas_anxiety_missing aas_total_missing ETTR_missing
0.0105263 0 0 0 0 0

For the IAS Scale, the AAS Scale along with multiple subscales including: closeness, dependence, anxiety, and total attachment, and ETRR Scale. Output displays Min., 1st Quartile, Median, Mean, 3rd Quartile, Max., and NA’s

Create Attachment Anxiety Groups

The different subscales for attachment are created here, including: closeness, dependence, anxiety, and total attachment.

Show code 
# Create median values for the attachment scale scores
aas_total_median <- median(study_data$aas_total_scale, na.rm = TRUE)
aas_close_median <- median(study_data$aas_close_scale, na.rm = TRUE)
aas_depend_median <- median(study_data$aas_depend_scale, na.rm = TRUE)
aas_anxiety_median <- median(study_data$aas_anxiety_scale, na.rm = TRUE)

# Create median-split attachment groups
study_data <- study_data %>%
  mutate(
    aas_total_group = if_else(aas_total_scale <= aas_total_median, "Lower attachment security", "Higher attachment security"),
    aas_total_group = factor(aas_total_group, levels = c("Lower attachment security", "Higher attachment security")),
    
    aas_close_group = if_else(aas_close_scale <= aas_close_median, "Lower closeness security", "Higher closeness security"),
    aas_close_group = factor(aas_close_group, levels = c("Lower closeness security", "Higher closeness security")),
    
    aas_depend_group = if_else(aas_depend_scale <= aas_depend_median, "Lower dependence security", "Higher dependence security"),
    aas_depend_group = factor(aas_depend_group, levels = c("Lower dependence security", "Higher dependence security")),
    
    aas_anxiety_group = if_else(aas_anxiety_scale <= aas_anxiety_median, "Lower anxiety security", "Higher anxiety security"),
    aas_anxiety_group = factor(aas_anxiety_group, levels = c("Lower anxiety security", "Higher anxiety security"))
  )

# Check final attachment group sizes
study_data %>%
  count(aas_total_group) %>%
  knitr::kable()
aas_total_group n
Lower attachment security 49
Higher attachment security 46
Show code 
study_data %>%
  count(aas_close_group) %>%
  knitr::kable()
aas_close_group n
Lower closeness security 48
Higher closeness security 47
Show code 
study_data %>%
  count(aas_depend_group) %>%
  knitr::kable()
aas_depend_group n
Lower dependence security 50
Higher dependence security 45
Show code 
study_data %>%
  count(aas_anxiety_group) %>%
  knitr::kable()
aas_anxiety_group n
Lower anxiety security 51
Higher anxiety security 44

Total: lower attachment security group had 49 participants, higher attachment security group had 46 participants

Closenss: lower closeness security group had 48 participants, higher closeness security group had 47 participants

Dependence: lower dependence security group had 50 participants, higher dependence security group had 45 participants

Anxiety: lower anxiety security group had 51 participants, higher anxiety security group had 44 participants

Check Scale Reliability

To check for reliability regarding the scales, the data frames were loaded in here to compute internal consistency

Show code 
# Create item-only data frames for each scale
IAS_item_df <- study_data %>% select(all_of(IAS_items))
aas_close_item_df <- study_data %>% select(all_of(aas_close_items))
aas_depend_item_df <- study_data %>% select(all_of(aas_depend_items))
aas_anxiety_item_df <- study_data %>% select(all_of(aas_anxiety_items))
aas_total_item_df <- study_data %>% select(all_of(aas_total_items))
ETTR_item_df <- study_data %>% select(all_of(ETTR_items))

# Compute internal consistency estimates
IAS_alpha <- psych::alpha(IAS_item_df)
IAS_omega <- psych::omega(IAS_item_df, plot = FALSE)

aas_close_alpha <- psych::alpha(aas_close_item_df)
aas_close_omega <- psych::omega(aas_close_item_df, plot = FALSE)

aas_depend_alpha <- psych::alpha(aas_depend_item_df)
aas_depend_omega <- psych::omega(aas_depend_item_df, plot = FALSE)

aas_anxiety_alpha <- psych::alpha(aas_anxiety_item_df)
aas_anxiety_omega <- psych::omega(aas_anxiety_item_df, plot = FALSE)

aas_total_alpha <- psych::alpha(aas_total_item_df)
aas_total_omega <- psych::omega(aas_total_item_df, plot = FALSE)

ETTR_alpha <- psych::alpha(ETTR_item_df)
ETTR_omega <- psych::omega(ETTR_item_df, plot = FALSE)

# Print only alpha and omega coefficients
tibble(
  scale = c("Social Attraction", "AAS Close Security", "AAS Depend Security", "AAS Anxiety Security", "AAS Total Security", "Relationship Expectation"),
  alpha = c(IAS_alpha$total$raw_alpha, aas_close_alpha$total$raw_alpha, aas_depend_alpha$total$raw_alpha, aas_anxiety_alpha$total$raw_alpha, aas_total_alpha$total$raw_alpha, ETTR_alpha$total$raw_alpha),
  omega = c(IAS_omega$omega.tot, aas_close_omega$omega.tot, aas_depend_omega$omega.tot, aas_anxiety_omega$omega.tot, aas_total_omega$omega.tot, ETTR_omega$omega.tot)
) %>%
  knitr::kable()
scale alpha omega
Social Attraction 0.8968806 0.9514521
AAS Close Security 0.7371310 0.8174746
AAS Depend Security 0.7550210 0.8630135
AAS Anxiety Security 0.8796060 0.9251593
AAS Total Security 0.8733301 0.9158943
Relationship Expectation 0.9643066 0.9726005

Internal consistency values are reported above, reporting Cronbach’s alpha and McDonald’s omega

Descriptive Statistics

Descriptive statistics regarding the scale scores are recorded here

Scale Score Descriptives

Show code 
study_data %>%
  select(
    IAS_scale,
    aas_close_scale,
    aas_depend_scale,
    aas_anxiety_scale,
    aas_total_scale,
    ETTR_scale
  ) %>%
  psych::describe() %>%
  knitr::kable(digits = 2)
vars n mean sd median trimmed mad min max range skew kurtosis se
IAS_scale 1 94 5.24 1.10 5.33 5.34 0.99 1.67 7.00 5.33 -0.97 1.25 0.11
aas_close_scale 2 95 3.36 0.75 3.33 3.36 0.74 1.00 5.00 4.00 -0.17 0.25 0.08
aas_depend_scale 3 95 2.84 0.78 2.83 2.85 0.74 1.17 4.83 3.67 -0.03 -0.50 0.08
aas_anxiety_scale 4 95 3.17 1.00 3.17 3.18 1.14 1.00 5.00 4.00 -0.04 -0.80 0.10
aas_total_scale 5 95 3.12 0.67 3.11 3.12 0.74 1.33 4.61 3.28 0.00 -0.51 0.07
ETTR_scale 6 95 3.88 1.00 4.05 3.91 1.17 1.79 5.89 4.11 -0.27 -0.94 0.10

The output describes the values for each scale, showing the vars., total participants, standard deviation, median, trimmed, mad, min., max, range, skew, kurtosis, and se

Sample Composition by Age, Gender, and Relationship

Show code 
study_data %>%
  count(age) %>%
  arrange(age) %>%
  mutate(percent = round(100 * n / sum(n), 1)) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("Age", "n", "Percent"),
    caption = "Sample Composition by Age"
  )
Sample Composition by Age
Age n Percent
18 2 2.1
19 5 5.3
20 11 11.6
21 35 36.8
22 30 31.6
23 3 3.2
24 1 1.1
26 2 2.1
42 1 1.1
NA 5 5.3
Show code 
study_data %>%
  count(gender) %>%
  arrange(gender) %>%
  mutate(percent = round(100 * n / sum(n), 1)) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("Gender", "n", "Percent"),
    caption = "Sample Composition by Gender"
  )
Sample Composition by Gender
Gender n Percent
0 1 1.1
1 23 24.2
2 70 73.7
3 1 1.1
Show code 
study_data %>%
  count(relationship) %>%
  arrange(relationship) %>%
  mutate(percent = round(100 * n / sum(n), 1)) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("Relationship", "n", "Percent"),
    caption = "Sample Composition by Year"
  )
Sample Composition by Year
Relationship n Percent
0 54 56.8
1 41 43.2

The output here describes the age range of particiapnts, which ranged from 18 - 24, gender of the participants, and if they are in a relationship or not

Correlations Among the DVs

Intercorrelations among the six composite scales are presented above. The dependent variables measured through the IAS and ETRR Scale were strongly correlated. The AAS subscales showed the expected pattern: Close and Depend security were positively intercorrelated, and both were negatively correlated with Anxiety security.

Show code 
# Correlations for the main scale scores
psych::corr.test(
  study_data %>% 
    select(
      IAS_scale,
      aas_close_scale,
      aas_depend_scale,
      aas_anxiety_scale,
      aas_total_scale,
      ETTR_scale
    )
)
Call:psych::corr.test(x = study_data %>% select(IAS_scale, aas_close_scale, 
    aas_depend_scale, aas_anxiety_scale, aas_total_scale, ETTR_scale))
Correlation matrix 
                  IAS_scale aas_close_scale aas_depend_scale aas_anxiety_scale
IAS_scale              1.00            0.25             0.27              0.33
aas_close_scale        0.25            1.00             0.64              0.40
aas_depend_scale       0.27            0.64             1.00              0.34
aas_anxiety_scale      0.33            0.40             0.34              1.00
aas_total_scale        0.36            0.82             0.79              0.78
ETTR_scale             0.71            0.15             0.14              0.19
                  aas_total_scale ETTR_scale
IAS_scale                    0.36       0.71
aas_close_scale              0.82       0.15
aas_depend_scale             0.79       0.14
aas_anxiety_scale            0.78       0.19
aas_total_scale              1.00       0.20
ETTR_scale                   0.20       1.00
Sample Size 
                  IAS_scale aas_close_scale aas_depend_scale aas_anxiety_scale
IAS_scale                94              94               94                94
aas_close_scale          94              95               95                95
aas_depend_scale         94              95               95                95
aas_anxiety_scale        94              95               95                95
aas_total_scale          94              95               95                95
ETTR_scale               94              95               95                95
                  aas_total_scale ETTR_scale
IAS_scale                      94         94
aas_close_scale                95         95
aas_depend_scale               95         95
aas_anxiety_scale              95         95
aas_total_scale                95         95
ETTR_scale                     95         95
Probability values (Entries above the diagonal are adjusted for multiple tests.) 
                  IAS_scale aas_close_scale aas_depend_scale aas_anxiety_scale
IAS_scale              0.00            0.08             0.04              0.01
aas_close_scale        0.02            0.00             0.00              0.00
aas_depend_scale       0.01            0.00             0.00              0.00
aas_anxiety_scale      0.00            0.00             0.00              0.00
aas_total_scale        0.00            0.00             0.00              0.00
ETTR_scale             0.00            0.16             0.18              0.07
                  aas_total_scale ETTR_scale
IAS_scale                    0.00       0.00
aas_close_scale              0.00       0.31
aas_depend_scale             0.00       0.31
aas_anxiety_scale            0.00       0.22
aas_total_scale              0.00       0.21
ETTR_scale                   0.05       0.00

 To see confidence intervals of the correlations, print with the short=FALSE option

Primary Analyses

ANOVA perfomed on relationship expectation scores

Relationship Expectation Model 1: Total Attachment Security

Show code 
# 2x2 between-subjects ANOVA predicting relationship expectation scores
# DV: ETTR_scale
# Factors: condition and aas_total_group
a1_ETTR_total <- afex::aov_ez(
  id = "id",
  dv = "ETTR_scale",
  between = c("condition", "aas_total_group"),
  data = study_data
)

# Print the ANOVA table
a1_ETTR_total
Anova Table (Type 3 tests)

Response: ETTR_scale
                     Effect    df  MSE         F  ges p.value
1                 condition 1, 91 0.75 32.97 *** .266   <.001
2           aas_total_group 1, 91 0.75      1.49 .016    .225
3 condition:aas_total_group 1, 91 0.75      0.17 .002    .680
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '+' 0.1 ' ' 1

The main effect of communication condition was significant

The main effect of attachment security groups was not significant

The interaction main effect between communication and attachment security was not significant

Estimated Means for Relationship Expectation: Total Attachment Security

Show code 
emmeans(a1_ETTR_total, ~ condition)
 condition emmean    SE df lower.CL upper.CL
 Low         3.35 0.129 91     3.09     3.61
 High        4.38 0.123 91     4.13     4.62

Results are averaged over the levels of: aas_total_group 
Confidence level used: 0.95
Show code 
emmeans(a1_ETTR_total, ~ aas_total_group)
 aas_total_group            emmean    SE df lower.CL upper.CL
 Lower attachment security    3.75 0.125 91     3.50     4.00
 Higher attachment security   3.97 0.128 91     3.72     4.23

Results are averaged over the levels of: condition 
Confidence level used: 0.95
Show code 
emmeans(a1_ETTR_total, ~ condition * aas_total_group)
 condition aas_total_group            emmean    SE df lower.CL upper.CL
 Low       Lower attachment security    3.28 0.189 91     2.90     3.65
 High      Lower attachment security    4.23 0.164 91     3.90     4.55
 Low       Higher attachment security   3.42 0.177 91     3.07     3.77
 High      Higher attachment security   4.52 0.185 91     4.15     4.89

Confidence level used: 0.95

The results are averages over the levels of condition

For the low condition lower attachment security, the estimated mean is 3.28

For the low condition higher attachment security, the estimated mean is 3.42

For the high condition lower attachment security, the estimated mean is 4.23

For the high condition higher attachment security, the estimated mean is 4.52

The estimated means for the high condition group is gnerally higher than the low condition group

Relationship Expectation Model 2: Closeness Security

Show code 
# 2x2 between-subjects ANOVA predicting relationship expectation scores
# DV: ETTR_scale
# Factors: condition and aas_close_group
a1_ETTR_close <- afex::aov_ez(
  id = "id",
  dv = "ETTR_scale",
  between = c("condition", "aas_close_group"),
  data = study_data
)

# Print the ANOVA table
a1_ETTR_close
Anova Table (Type 3 tests)

Response: ETTR_scale
                     Effect    df  MSE         F   ges p.value
1                 condition 1, 91 0.75 33.37 ***  .268   <.001
2           aas_close_group 1, 91 0.75      2.00  .021    .161
3 condition:aas_close_group 1, 91 0.75      0.03 <.001    .853
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '+' 0.1 ' ' 1

The main effect of communication condition is significant

The main effect of attachment close security groups was not significant

The interaction main effect between communication and attachment was not significant

Estimated Means for Relationship Expectation: Closeness Security

Show code 
emmeans(a1_ETTR_close, ~ condition)
 condition emmean    SE df lower.CL upper.CL
 Low         3.34 0.130 91     3.08     3.60
 High        4.37 0.123 91     4.13     4.62

Results are averaged over the levels of: aas_close_group 
Confidence level used: 0.95
Show code 
emmeans(a1_ETTR_close, ~ aas_close_group)
 aas_close_group           emmean    SE df lower.CL upper.CL
 Lower closeness security    3.73 0.127 91     3.48     3.98
 Higher closeness security   3.98 0.126 91     3.73     4.23

Results are averaged over the levels of: condition 
Confidence level used: 0.95
Show code 
emmeans(a1_ETTR_close, ~ condition * aas_close_group)
 condition aas_close_group           emmean    SE df lower.CL upper.CL
 Low       Lower closeness security    3.19 0.193 91     2.81     3.58
 High      Lower closeness security    4.26 0.163 91     3.94     4.59
 Low       Higher closeness security   3.48 0.173 91     3.14     3.82
 High      Higher closeness security   4.48 0.184 91     4.11     4.85

Confidence level used: 0.95

The results are averages over the levels of condition

For the low condition lower closeness security, the estimated mean is 3.19

For the low condition higher closeness security, the estimated mean is 3.48

For the high condition lower closeness security, the estimated mean is 4.26

For the high condition higher closeness security, the estimated mean is 4.48

The estimated means for the high condition group is gnerally higher than the low condition group

Relationship Expectation Model 3: Dependence Security

Show code 
# 2x2 between-subjects ANOVA predicting relationship expectation scores
# DV: ETTR_scale
# Factors: condition and aas_depend_group
a1_ETTR_depend <- afex::aov_ez(
  id = "id",
  dv = "ETTR_scale",
  between = c("condition", "aas_depend_group"),
  data = study_data
)

# Print the ANOVA table
a1_ETTR_depend
Anova Table (Type 3 tests)

Response: ETTR_scale
                      Effect    df  MSE         F  ges p.value
1                  condition 1, 91 0.74 32.98 *** .266   <.001
2           aas_depend_group 1, 91 0.74      1.74 .019    .190
3 condition:aas_depend_group 1, 91 0.74      0.75 .008    .388
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '+' 0.1 ' ' 1

The main effect of communication condition was significant

The main effect of attachment dependence security groups was not significant

The interaction main effect between communication and attachment depedence was not significant

Estimated Means for Relationship Expectation: Dependence Security

Show code 
emmeans(a1_ETTR_depend, ~ condition)
 condition emmean    SE df lower.CL upper.CL
 Low         3.35 0.129 91     3.10     3.61
 High        4.37 0.122 91     4.13     4.62

Results are averaged over the levels of: aas_depend_group 
Confidence level used: 0.95
Show code 
emmeans(a1_ETTR_depend, ~ aas_depend_group)
 aas_depend_group           emmean    SE df lower.CL upper.CL
 Lower dependence security    3.75 0.122 91     3.50     3.99
 Higher dependence security   3.98 0.129 91     3.73     4.24

Results are averaged over the levels of: condition 
Confidence level used: 0.95
Show code 
emmeans(a1_ETTR_depend, ~ condition * aas_depend_group)
 condition aas_depend_group           emmean    SE df lower.CL upper.CL
 Low       Lower dependence security    3.31 0.180 91     2.96     3.67
 High      Lower dependence security    4.18 0.166 91     3.85     4.51
 Low       Higher dependence security   3.39 0.184 91     3.03     3.76
 High      Higher dependence security   4.57 0.180 91     4.21     4.92

Confidence level used: 0.95

The results are averages over the levels of condition

For the low condition lower dependence security, the estimated mean is 3.31

For the low condition higher dependence security, the estimated mean is 3.39

For the high condition lower dependence security, the estimated mean is 4.18

For the high condition higher dependence security, the estimated mean is 4.47

# The estimated means for the high condition group is gnerally higher than the low condition group

Relationship Expectation Model 4: Anxiety Security

Show code 
# 2x2 between-subjects ANOVA predicting relationship expectation scores
# DV: ETTR_scale
# Factors: condition and aas_anxiety_group
a1_ETTR_anxiety <- afex::aov_ez(
  id = "id",
  dv = "ETTR_scale",
  between = c("condition", "aas_anxiety_group"),
  data = study_data
)

# Print the ANOVA table
a1_ETTR_anxiety
Anova Table (Type 3 tests)

Response: ETTR_scale
                       Effect    df  MSE         F   ges p.value
1                   condition 1, 91 0.75 29.19 ***  .243   <.001
2           aas_anxiety_group 1, 91 0.75      1.90  .020    .172
3 condition:aas_anxiety_group 1, 91 0.75      0.05 <.001    .824
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '+' 0.1 ' ' 1

The output here shows a significant main effect of communication condition

The main effect of attachment anxiety condition was significant

The interaction main effect between communication and attachment anxiety was not significant

Estimated Means for Relationship Expectation: Anxiety Security

Show code 
emmeans(a1_ETTR_anxiety, ~ condition)
 condition emmean    SE df lower.CL upper.CL
 Low         3.38 0.132 91     3.12     3.64
 High        4.35 0.122 91     4.11     4.60

Results are averaged over the levels of: aas_anxiety_group 
Confidence level used: 0.95
Show code 
emmeans(a1_ETTR_anxiety, ~ aas_anxiety_group)
 aas_anxiety_group       emmean    SE df lower.CL upper.CL
 Lower anxiety security    3.74 0.121 91     3.50     3.99
 Higher anxiety security   3.99 0.133 91     3.73     4.26

Results are averaged over the levels of: condition 
Confidence level used: 0.95
Show code 
emmeans(a1_ETTR_anxiety, ~ condition * aas_anxiety_group)
 condition aas_anxiety_group       emmean    SE df lower.CL upper.CL
 Low       Lower anxiety security    3.24 0.166 91     2.91     3.57
 High      Lower anxiety security    4.25 0.177 91     3.90     4.60
 Low       Higher anxiety security   3.53 0.204 91     3.12     3.93
 High      Higher anxiety security   4.46 0.170 91     4.12     4.79

Confidence level used: 0.95

The results are averages over the levels of condition

For the low condition lower anxiety security, the estimated mean is 3.24

For the low condition higher anxiety security, the estimated mean is 3.53

For the high condition lower anxiety security, the estimated mean is 4.25

For the high condition higher anxiety security, the estimated mean is 4.46

The estimated means for the high condition group is gnerally higher than the low condition group

Social Attraction Model 1: Total Attachment Security

Show code 
# 2x2 between-subjects ANOVA predicting social attraction scores
# DV: IAS_scale
# Factors: condition and aas_total_group
a1_IAS_total <- afex::aov_ez(
  id = "id",
  dv = "IAS_scale",
  between = c("condition", "aas_total_group"),
  data = study_data
)

# Print the ANOVA table
a1_IAS_total
Anova Table (Type 3 tests)

Response: IAS_scale
                     Effect    df  MSE         F  ges p.value
1                 condition 1, 90 1.01 16.51 *** .155   <.001
2           aas_total_group 1, 90 1.01   7.48 ** .077    .008
3 condition:aas_total_group 1, 90 1.01      0.36 .004    .551
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '+' 0.1 ' ' 1

The output here shows a significant main effect of communication condition

The main effect of attachment security condition was significant

The interaction main effect between communication and attachment security was not significant

Estimated Means for Social Attraction: Total Attachment Security

Show code 
emmeans(a1_IAS_total, ~ condition)
 condition emmean    SE df lower.CL upper.CL
 Low         4.79 0.152 90     4.49     5.09
 High        5.64 0.143 90     5.36     5.92

Results are averaged over the levels of: aas_total_group 
Confidence level used: 0.95
Show code 
emmeans(a1_IAS_total, ~ aas_total_group)
 aas_total_group            emmean    SE df lower.CL upper.CL
 Lower attachment security    4.93 0.147 90     4.64     5.22
 Higher attachment security   5.50 0.148 90     5.21     5.80

Results are averaged over the levels of: condition 
Confidence level used: 0.95
Show code 
emmeans(a1_IAS_total, ~ condition * aas_total_group)
 condition aas_total_group            emmean    SE df lower.CL upper.CL
 Low       Lower attachment security    4.44 0.225 90     3.99     4.89
 High      Lower attachment security    5.42 0.190 90     5.04     5.79
 Low       Higher attachment security   5.14 0.205 90     4.73     5.55
 High      Higher attachment security   5.86 0.215 90     5.44     6.29

Confidence level used: 0.95

The results are averages over the levels of condition

For the low condition lower attachment security, the estimated mean is 4.44

For the low condition higher attachment security, the estimated mean is 5.14

For the high condition lower attachment security, the estimated mean is 5.42

For the high condition higher attachment security, the estimated mean is 5.86

The estimated means for the high condition group is gnerally higher than the low condition group

Social Attraction Model 2: Closeness Security

Show code 
# 2x2 between-subjects ANOVA predicting social attraction scores
# DV: IAS_scale
# Factors: condition and aas_close_group
a1_IAS_close <- afex::aov_ez(
  id = "id",
  dv = "IAS_scale",
  between = c("condition", "aas_close_group"),
  data = study_data
)

# Print the ANOVA table
a1_IAS_close
Anova Table (Type 3 tests)

Response: IAS_scale
                     Effect    df  MSE         F   ges p.value
1                 condition 1, 90 1.04 15.66 ***  .148   <.001
2           aas_close_group 1, 90 1.04    4.80 *  .051    .031
3 condition:aas_close_group 1, 90 1.04      0.01 <.001    .932
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '+' 0.1 ' ' 1

The output here shows a significant main effect of communication condition

The main effect of attachment closeness condition was significant

The interaction main effect between communication and attachment closeness was not significant

Estimated Means for Social Attraction: Closeness Security

Show code 
emmeans(a1_IAS_close, ~ condition)
 condition emmean    SE df lower.CL upper.CL
 Low         4.80 0.155 90     4.49     5.11
 High        5.64 0.145 90     5.35     5.93

Results are averaged over the levels of: aas_close_group 
Confidence level used: 0.95
Show code

 Higher closeness security   5.45 0.151 90     5.15     5.75

Results are averaged over the levels of: condition 
Confidence level used: 0.95
Show code 
emmeans(a1_IAS_close, ~ condition * aas_close_group)
 condition aas_close_group           emmean    SE df lower.CL upper.CL
 Low       Lower closeness security    4.56 0.228 90     4.10     5.01
 High      Lower closeness security    5.42 0.193 90     5.03     5.80
 Low       Higher closeness security   5.04 0.208 90     4.63     5.46
 High      Higher closeness security   5.86 0.218 90     5.43     6.30

The results are averages over the levels of condition

For the low condition lower closeness security, the estimated mean is 4.56

For the low condition higher closeness security, the estimated mean is 5.04

For the high condition lower closeness security, the estimated mean is 5.42

For the high condition higher closeness security, the estimated mean is 5.86

Social Attraction Model 3: Dependence Security

Show code 
# 2x2 between-subjects ANOVA predicting social attraction scores
# DV: IAS_scale
# Factors: condition and aas_depend_group
a1_IAS_depend <- afex::aov_ez(
  id = "id",
  dv = "IAS_scale",
  between = c("condition", "aas_depend_group"),
  data = study_data
)

# Print the ANOVA table
a1_IAS_depend
Anova Table (Type 3 tests)

Response: IAS_scale
                      Effect    df  MSE         F  ges p.value
1                  condition 1, 90 1.00 15.31 *** .145   <.001
2           aas_depend_group 1, 90 1.00   8.45 ** .086    .005
3 condition:aas_depend_group 1, 90 1.00      0.24 .003    .625
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '+' 0.1 ' ' 1

The output here shows a significant main effect of communication condition

The main effect of attachment dependent condition was significant

The interaction main effect between communication and attachment dependent was not significant

Estimated Means for Social Attraction: Dependence Security

Show code 
emmeans(a1_IAS_depend, ~ condition)
 condition emmean    SE df lower.CL upper.CL
 Low         4.82 0.151 90     4.52     5.12
 High        5.63 0.142 90     5.35     5.92

Results are averaged over the levels of: aas_depend_group 
Confidence level used: 0.95
Show code 
emmeans(a1_IAS_depend, ~ aas_depend_group)
 aas_depend_group           emmean    SE df lower.CL upper.CL
 Lower dependence security    4.93 0.144 90     4.64     5.21
 Higher dependence security   5.53 0.149 90     5.23     5.83

Results are averaged over the levels of: condition 
Confidence level used: 0.95
Show code 
emmeans(a1_IAS_depend, ~ condition * aas_depend_group)
 condition aas_depend_group           emmean    SE df lower.CL upper.CL
 Low       Lower dependence security    4.47 0.214 90     4.05     4.89
 High      Lower dependence security    5.38 0.193 90     5.00     5.77
 Low       Higher dependence security   5.17 0.214 90     4.75     5.60
 High      Higher dependence security   5.88 0.209 90     5.47     6.30

Confidence level used: 0.95

The results are averages over the levels of condition

For the low condition lower dependence security, the estimated mean is 4.47

For the low condition higher dependence security, the estimated mean is 5.17

For the high condition lower dependence security, the estimated mean is 5.38

For the high condition higher dependence security, the estimated mean is 5.88

The estimated means for the high condition group is gnerally higher than the low condition group

Social Attraction Model 4: Anxiety Security

Show code 
# 2x2 between-subjects ANOVA predicting social attraction scores
# DV: IAS_scale
# Factors: condition and aas_anxiety_group
a1_IAS_anxiety <- afex::aov_ez(
  id = "id",
  dv = "IAS_scale",
  between = c("condition", "aas_anxiety_group"),
  data = study_data
)

# Print the ANOVA table
a1_IAS_anxiety
Anova Table (Type 3 tests)

Response: IAS_scale
                       Effect    df  MSE         F  ges p.value
1                   condition 1, 90 1.02 11.80 *** .116   <.001
2           aas_anxiety_group 1, 90 1.02    6.66 * .069    .012
3 condition:aas_anxiety_group 1, 90 1.02      0.17 .002    .685
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '+' 0.1 ' ' 1

The output here shows a significant main effect of communication condition

The main effect of attachment anxiety condition was significant

The interaction main effect between communication and attachment anxiety was not significant

Estimated Means for Social Attraction: Anxiety Security

Show code 
emmeans(a1_IAS_anxiety, ~ condition)
 condition emmean    SE df lower.CL upper.CL
 Low         4.88 0.155 90     4.57     5.19
 High        5.60 0.143 90     5.32     5.89

Results are averaged over the levels of: aas_anxiety_group 
Confidence level used: 0.95
Show code 
emmeans(a1_IAS_anxiety, ~ aas_anxiety_group)
 aas_anxiety_group       emmean    SE df lower.CL upper.CL
 Lower anxiety security    4.97 0.143 90     4.69     5.25
 Higher anxiety security   5.51 0.155 90     5.21     5.82

Results are averaged over the levels of: condition 
Confidence level used: 0.95
Show code 
emmeans(a1_IAS_anxiety, ~ condition * aas_anxiety_group)
 condition aas_anxiety_group       emmean    SE df lower.CL upper.CL
 Low       Lower anxiety security    4.56 0.198 90     4.17     4.96
 High      Lower anxiety security    5.38 0.206 90     4.96     5.79
 Low       Higher anxiety security   5.19 0.238 90     4.72     5.67
 High      Higher anxiety security   5.83 0.198 90     5.44     6.23

Confidence level used: 0.95

The results are averages over the levels of condition

For the low condition lower anxiety security, the estimated mean is 4.56

For the low condition higher anxiety security, the estimated mean is 5.19

For the high condition lower anxiety security, the estimated mean is 5.38

For the high condition higher anxiety security, the estimated mean is 5.83

The estimated means for the high condition group is gnerally higher than the low condition group

Figures

Below are the bar graphs for each measure along with the subscale scores

Relationship Expectation Interaction Plot: Total Attachment Security

Show code 
p_ETTR_total_bar <- ggplot(
  as.data.frame(emmeans(a1_ETTR_total, ~ condition * aas_total_group)),
  aes(
    x = condition,
    y = emmean,
    fill = aas_total_group
  )
) +
  geom_col(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.65),
    width = 0.55,
    color = "white"
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(
      ymin = lower.CL,
      ymax = upper.CL
    ),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.65),
    width = 0.12,
    linewidth = 0.7
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Communication Condition",
    y = "Mean Relationship Expectation Score",
    fill = "Total Attachment Security"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "Lower attachment security" = "#FFC0CB",   
      "Higher attachment security" = "#89CFF0"   
    )
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    breaks = 1:6
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(
    ylim = c(1, 6)
  ) +
  theme_classic(
    base_size = 14
  )

p_ETTR_total_bar

There does not appear to be an interaction

Relationship Expectation Interaction Plot: Closeness Security

Show code 
p_ETTR_close_bar <- ggplot(
  as.data.frame(emmeans(a1_ETTR_close, ~ condition * aas_close_group)),

    y = emmean,
    fill = aas_close_group

) +
  geom_col(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.65),

Save FIgures

Show code 
if (!dir.exists("figures")) {
  dir.create("figures")
}

ggsave(
  filename = "figures/relationship_expectation_total_attachment_security_interaction_plot.svg",
  plot = p_ETTR_total_bar,
  width = 7,
  height = 5
)

ggsave(
  filename = "figures/relationship_expectation_closeness_security_interaction_plot.svg",
  plot = p_ETTR_close_bar,
  width = 7,
  height = 5
)

ggsave(
  filename = "figures/relationship_expectation_dependence_security_interaction_plot.svg",
  plot = p_ETTR_depend_bar,
  width = 7,
  height = 5
)

ggsave(
  filename = "figures/relationship_expectation_anxiety_security_interaction_plot.svg",
  plot = p_ETTR_anxiety_bar,
  width = 7,
  height = 5
)

ggsave(
  filename = "figures/social_attraction_total_attachment_security_interaction_plot.svg",
  plot = p_IAS_total_bar,
  width = 7,
  height = 5
)

ggsave(
  filename = "figures/social_attraction_closeness_security_interaction_plot.svg",
  plot = p_IAS_close_bar,
  width = 7,
  height = 5
)

ggsave(
  filename = "figures/social_attraction_dependence_security_interaction_plot.svg",
  plot = p_IAS_depend_bar,
  width = 7,
  height = 5
)

ggsave(
  filename = "figures/social_attraction_anxiety_security_interaction_plot.svg",
  plot = p_IAS_anxiety_bar,
  width = 7,
  height = 5
)

Results Summary

While there are multipel ANOVAs conducted in this statistical analysis report, this study only looks at communication x attachment security on perceived compatability and communication x attachment security on social attraction.

For the first ANOVA, the results revealed a significant main effect of communication, no significant main effect of attachment security, and no significant interaction effect.

For the second ANOVA, the results revealed a significant main effect of communication, a significant main effect of attachment security, but no significant interaction effect.

Reproducibility Information

This specific R version, operating system, and package versions were employed to compile the statistical analysis reporty in this section. Sharing these results allows for readers to recreate the exact computational context in which the analyses were performed.

This section records the R version, operating system, and package versions used to render the report. Including this information helps another reader understand the software environment used to reproduce the analyses.
